title: Screen Position Jumps
subtitle: {key:H}, {key:M}, {key:L} — High, Middle, Low on the visible screen.
summary: {key:H} jumps to the top of the visible screen, {key:M} to the middle, {key:L} to the bottom. They're motions to a *screen* position, not a *file* position — they don't scroll.

blocks.text: Three single-key jumps to positions on the *current screen*: top, middle, bottom. They don't scroll — they move the cursor to where you can already see.

blocks.title: These Are Window-Local
blocks.text:
{key:H}, {key:M}, {key:L} operate on the *visible* lines of the current window. They have nothing to do with file lines or absolute positions. So they behave the same in a small split as in a full-screen window.

They respect `scrolloff` — if you have `:set scrolloff=5`, {key:H} lands on the 6th visible line (5 lines below the actual top), and {key:L} lands 5 above the actual bottom. This keeps context visible.

With a count, {key:5}{key:H} means "5 lines below the top of the screen"; {key:5}{key:L} means "5 lines above the bottom." Useful for jumping into a known-position landmark on screen.

These are *jumps* — they push the previous position onto the jumplist. {key:Ctrl-O} returns.

{key:H}/{key:M}/{key:L} are paired with {key:z}{key:t}/{key:z}{key:z}/{key:z}{key:b} (which scroll the cursor's *current* line to the top/middle/bottom of the screen; see [*Centering the Cursor*](#scroll.center-top-bottom)). Together they let you reposition either the cursor or the viewport without thinking.
